pages.title: Cyber Risk Quantification at KPMG
pages.content:
I didn’t take the traditional path into tech — or into anything, really. I spent the first half of my life outrunning feedback and building a professional shell around personal instability. That changed when I stopped drinking at 37. The hardest part wasn’t quitting alcohol. It was rebuilding an identity from zero — one grounded in honesty, clarity, and execution. That reset eventually became the foundation for the most important achievement of my career.

In 2012, I committed to a complete reinvention. I chose cybersecurity as my new field, filled the gaps in my math and programming skills, and relocated to the Netherlands for a master’s in information security. I had no local network — just an EU passport and two bags. The plan was 12 months. It’s been 12 years.

KPMG Netherlands hired me straight out of grad school. Within three years, I led the development of a proprietary cyber risk quantification model that improved on FAIR (The Open Group’s Factor Analysis of Information Risk), the industry standard. We replaced one-off complexity with reusable structure: measure once, apply many times. Our model clarified assumptions, reduced duplication, and turned risk into a strategic input rather than a compliance artifact.

That work scaled. I organized a working group across eight KPMG member firms on three continents, aligning global teams on a shared framework for cyber risk. Five years later, they still meet. The work not only improved internal efficiency but helped client CISOs make faster, more confident decisions in board-level risk discussions.

I’ve since moved on, but the experience taught me this: reinvention is repeatable, and technical leadership compounds. I’ve done it once — under pressure, in a new country, after personal collapse. Now I’m doing it again, building something new at 55 with more clarity, energy, and conviction than ever before. I don’t see age as a constraint. I see it as leverage.

posts.title: False Positives, False Negatives, and the Real Calibration Problem
posts.excerpt: Any system that looks for research weaknesses will make mistakes. The real question is not whether errors occur, but whether they are visible, measurable, correctable, and tied to a versioned record rather than hidden inside prestige or private review.
posts.content:
Any system that evaluates papers for reliability will make mistakes.

That includes Referee.

This is not embarrassing. It would be embarrassing to pretend otherwise.

A reliability system will sometimes flag a weakness that should not have been flagged. It will sometimes miss a weakness that was really there. It will sometimes apply a category too broadly. It will sometimes depend on a weak extraction. It will sometimes behave differently across fields, formats, and paper types.

The serious question is not whether errors exist.

The serious question is whether those errors are visible, measurable, correctable, and preserved in a form that improves the next version of the system.

Traditional peer review also has false positives and false negatives. It just hides them better.

Peer review is not the control group people imagine

A false positive in peer review occurs when a reviewer treats something as a serious flaw when it is not. The reviewer may misunderstand the method, apply a personal preference as if it were a requirement, or demand work that is irrelevant to the claim being made.

A false negative occurs when a reviewer misses a real problem. The review may be rushed. The wrong reviewer may have been selected. The statistical issue may fall outside the reviewer’s expertise. A private comment may be too soft. An editor may ignore it.

None of this is rare.

The mythology of peer review often compares new systems to a fantasy of patient, consistent, broad-spectrum expert judgment. That fantasy is not the baseline. The actual baseline is overburdened reviewers, uneven expertise, inconsistent incentives, hidden reports, and a published record that usually reveals very little about what was actually checked.

Referee does not create the calibration problem. It makes it inspectable.

False positives are product failures, not cosmetic bugs

False positives matter because they waste attention and distort trust.

If the system incorrectly flags a paper, it can unfairly reduce confidence in the claim, frustrate authors, and teach users to ignore the output. A reliability system with persistent false positives will quickly become noise.

That means false positives have to be classified and studied, not waved away.

A false positive can arise because the PDF extraction was bad. It can arise because a weakness category was applied outside its proper scope. It can arise because the evidence was real but irrelevant. It can arise because a prompt pushed the model toward over-reading ambiguity. It can arise because a deterministic processor was run at the edge of its assumptions.

These are different error families. The system should know the difference.

A project that classifies weaknesses in papers but not its own failure modes is not serious about calibration.

False negatives are quieter and often worse

False negatives are usually less visible.

When the system misses a weakness, the output may simply look clean. The score may appear reassuring. Downstream users may rely on the paper without realizing that an important problem was never detected.

That is why false negatives can be more dangerous than false positives.

A false positive triggers argument. A false negative often disappears into confidence.

This is one reason Referee should disclose scope aggressively. Users need to know what was checked, what was not checked, what evidence sources were available, and which CRWE families were actually in play. “No weakness triggered” is not the same claim as “this entire paper has been exhaustively validated.”

A score without scope disclosure invites invisible false negatives.

Calibration is not the same as caution

One tempting response to false positives is extreme caution.

That sounds responsible. It is not obviously responsible.

If the threshold for recording a weakness becomes too high, the system simply reproduces the academic habit of withholding criticism unless the case is overwhelming. The output becomes polite, conservative, and not very useful.

The opposite mistake is aggression. If the threshold becomes too low, users drown in pseudo-flaws and the score becomes punitive.

Calibration is the discipline of managing that tradeoff.

But caution should not be confused with indecision. Referee should still force a yes or no call on named CRWE items. The project does not solve calibration by inventing a third bucket called “unresolved” and hiding its uncertainty there. It solves calibration by preserving the uncertainty around the call: scope, evidence quality, disagreement, versioning, and challenge history.

That is a much cleaner design.

Narrow tasks improve calibration

One reason Referee can do better than open-ended paper review is that the tasks are narrower.

The system is not trying to replace the idealized all-knowing peer reviewer. That person does not exist anyway.

It is asking smaller questions tied to named weaknesses. Was a required disclosure absent? Were counts inconsistent? Was a leakage pattern described? Was the reported value implausible? Was the analysis incompatible with the stated design?

Narrow tasks are easier to benchmark, easier to improve, and easier to distribute across AI systems and non-academic contributors. That is a major advantage over traditional peer review, where a reviewer is often expected to span multiple specialties with little time and little accountability.

Multi-model votes help, but they are not magic

Using more than one model can reduce some errors.

One model may hallucinate. Another may miss an inconsistency. A third may interpret the definition more conservatively. Independent votes can reveal disagreement that a single model would hide.

But multi-model voting is not a solution by itself.

Models can share blind spots. They can be misled by the same ambiguity. A bad prompt can steer all of them in the same direction. Category definitions can still be weak.

The value of multiple votes is not that they produce truth. The value is that they produce more signal. A unanimous call is different from a split call. A finding supported by deterministic evidence is different from one resting only on interpretation.

Disagreement is information. It is not an excuse to stop judging.

Deterministic checks reduce some error classes

Referee should use deterministic enrichment wherever it can.

If reported counts can be reconciled, reconcile them. If numerical values can be checked for internal consistency, check them. If physiological measures can be compared with plausible ranges, compare them. If data-splitting claims can be checked against obvious leakage patterns, check them.

These checks do not eliminate judgment, but they move some parts of the system away from vibes and toward computation.

That improves calibration in two ways. It catches some problems that prose review misses, and it reduces the number of weaknesses that depend entirely on interpretive language models.

The audit trail is the correction engine

The most important design choice is to preserve the trail.

When Referee gets a paper wrong, the system should be able to answer concrete questions. Which text was extracted? Which weakness definition was applied? Which evidence snippet mattered? Which deterministic processor ran? Which models voted? Which scoring version was active? What later challenge or correction changed the result?

Without that trail, correction turns into guesswork.

With it, mistakes become training material. A false positive can improve a prompt, a category definition, a scope rule, a processor, or the weighting vector. A false negative can expose a missing weakness family, weak extraction, poor evidence schema, or unsupported paper type.

That is how a transparent system compounds improvement.

The score should expose uncertainty without avoiding judgment

A reliability score should not pretend to be more precise than the underlying evaluation.

If the paper was scored only from paper text, that should be visible. If code was not inspected, that should be visible. If only a subset of CRWE families was in scope, that should be visible. If the model votes were split, that should be visible. If the category definition is new and not yet well benchmarked, that should be visible.

But none of those disclosures requires an “unresolved” scoring state.

Uncertainty belongs in metadata around the call, not in place of the call.

That distinction matters because systems improve when they commit to explicit findings and then expose the reasons those findings may later change.

The real comparison is the current system

The comparison is not Referee versus perfect human review.

Perfect human review does not exist.

The comparison is Referee versus the system we actually have: rushed reviewers, narrow incentives, hidden reports, inconsistent standards, thin post-publication correction, and a scholarly record that usually carries almost no structured reliability information.

Against that baseline, false positives and false negatives are not arguments for abandoning public reliability scoring. They are arguments for building the scoring system in the open, with named categories, binary calls, scope disclosure, versioning, challenge mechanisms, and audit trails.

A flawed transparent system can improve because its errors are legible.

A flawed opaque system mostly asks for continued trust.

That is the real calibration problem.

posts.title: Why Reliability Should Be Machine-Readable
posts.excerpt: Scholarship is searched, ranked, funded, cited, regulated, and reused by systems. Reliability information locked in prose cannot travel through those channels. Referee turns reliability into structured metadata that can move with the paper.
posts.content:
A brilliant criticism trapped in prose is still trapped.

It may persuade the few people who read it. It may circulate through seminars, private conversations, PubPeer threads, blog posts, or commentaries. It may even become famous inside a specialty.

But it still may not travel with the paper.

That is a serious defect in the scholarly record.

Modern scholarship is not just read by specialists one paper at a time. It is searched, ranked, summarized, funded, cited, litigated, regulated, translated into policy, and absorbed into other systems. If reliability information cannot move through those same channels, then reliability remains peripheral while prestige does the real work.

That is why Referee makes reliability machine-readable.

Prose criticism does not scale

The traditional form of research criticism is prose.

A reviewer writes a report. A journal publishes a comment. A critic writes an essay. A reader posts a thread. An institution issues a statement. A retraction notice appears years later.

Some of this prose is excellent. Some of it is sloppy. Some of it is malicious. Some of it is indispensable.

Almost all of it is structurally hard to use.

Prose criticism is difficult to index, difficult to compare, difficult to search, difficult to update, and difficult to attach cleanly to the paper it discusses. It often does not use standardized categories. It often does not state what was checked. It often mixes direct defects, missing disclosures, interpretive disagreement, and reputational argument into the same paragraph.

The result is backwards. The scholarly record is often better at preserving the existence of a paper than at preserving structured information about its reliability.

Reliability should become metadata

Every paper already carries metadata.

Title. Authors. Journal. DOI. Publication date. Affiliations. Citations. Keywords. Funding disclosures. Sometimes data availability. Sometimes code availability. Sometimes badges.

This metadata is not decorative. It shapes discovery, ranking, and trust.

A DOI makes a paper easier to find. Citation counts increase visibility. A prestige journal changes how readers treat the paper before reading a word of it.

Yet one of the most important kinds of metadata is usually missing: what reliability-relevant weaknesses were checked, which ones were triggered, what evidence supported those findings, what scope limitations applied, and how the score was computed.

Referee’s position is simple. Reliability should be metadata too.

Not gossip. Not private review. Not scattered commentary. Structured, portable, inspectable metadata.

Machine-readable does not mean blind obedience

There is an obvious misunderstanding to avoid.

Machine-readable does not mean machine-sovereign. It does not mean software issues a verdict and humans are expected to obey. It does not mean readers stop exercising judgment. It does not mean the system cannot be contested.

It means the reliability record is structured enough to be stored, searched, filtered, versioned, compared, and updated.

That structure can still preserve evidence excerpts, scope notes, disagreement patterns, and challenge history. Humans can still dispute a finding. Authors can still provide new evidence. Later versions can still revise the score.

The point is not to eliminate judgment. The point is to stop trapping judgment in formats that do not travel.

Binary structure is what makes portability possible

This is one reason Referee forces yes or no calls on named CRWE items.

Unstructured prose is difficult to aggregate. “Unresolved concerns” is difficult to compare across papers. A binary weakness vector tied to definitions is much easier to store and reuse. Once the record is structured, software can do useful things with it.

It can answer questions such as these.

Which papers in this field triggered major audit-gap categories? Which papers changed score after code was released? Which papers cleared a given cluster of reproducibility checks? Which papers were scored under an outdated taxonomy version? Which high-citation papers still carry serious reliability flags?

Those are the kinds of queries the current scholarly record handles badly because the reliability layer has never really been built.

Search, ranking, and synthesis should know reliability

Right now most search systems know popularity much better than reliability.

They can rank by citation count, date, keyword relevance, journal, and author. They can count attention. They can track influence.

They usually cannot tell you which evidence base is fragile.

That is a major gap, especially outside academia. Policymakers, investors, journalists, clinicians, regulators, nonprofits, and civic users often encounter research through search, synthesis, or summary layers rather than by reading the whole literature in sequence.

Those users need more than prestige signals. They need a way to see where the literature appears structurally weak, where it appears stronger, and how those judgments were produced.

Referee is being built for that broader social use case. Academia is one user. It is not the whole audience.

Structured reliability is useful beyond journals

A journal editor may use reliability metadata one way. A replication team may use it another way. An investor performing scientific diligence may use it differently again. A policy team deciding whether to rely on a body of evidence may use it differently yet again.

What all of these users have in common is that they cannot start from zero every time.

They need a reliability layer that can travel with the paper and survive across workflows.

That is exactly what machine-readable structure enables. It lets a paper carry more than title, venue, and citation count. It lets a paper carry a versioned record of what weaknesses were checked, what was found, and what later changed.

Versioning is part of trust

Reliability records should change when the evidence changes.

A paper may release code later. A later extraction may fix a false positive. The taxonomy may improve. A new weakness category may be added. A challenge may succeed. A correction may appear.

When the score changes, the record should show why.

This is much easier when the reliability layer is structured. A machine-readable record can preserve score history, taxonomy version, scoring version, evidence scope, and timestamps. A static paragraph in prose cannot do that nearly as well.

Versioning is not a technical afterthought. It is part of trust. A system that updates without explanation is opaque. A system that updates with a visible trail earns the right to be challenged and improved.

Criticism becomes infrastructure when it can travel

Much research criticism evaporates.

A reviewer notices something and writes it in a confidential report. A reader notices a flaw and mentions it in conversation. A critic posts a valuable observation somewhere disconnected from the main scholarly workflow. A correction appears too late to reach the institutions that already acted on the original result.

Referee is built around a different idea: criticism should become infrastructure.

Once a criticism is mapped to a named weakness category, tied to evidence, attached to versioning, and made portable, it no longer lives only as prose. It becomes part of the paper’s public record.

That is a much more useful form of criticism.

Reliability should travel with the paper

The scholarly world has spent decades improving identifiers, indexing, citation tracking, and discovery.

It has invested far less in making reliability travel with the objects being discovered.

That imbalance no longer makes sense.

If a paper is important enough to be funded, cited, commercialized, regulated, or used as evidence for public decisions, then it is important enough to carry structured reliability metadata.

Not as a final truth machine. Not as a substitute for reading. Not as a new priesthood.

As a public, inspectable layer that moves with the paper wherever the paper goes.

That is why reliability should be machine-readable.

posts.title: Defect or Audit Gap? Why Referee Forces a Call Anyway
posts.excerpt: Research criticism usually collapses actual flaws and missing information into one vague complaint. Referee separates defects from audit gaps and then forces explicit yes/no calls on both. Ambiguity is preserved in the evidence record, not promoted into a third scoring state.
posts.content:
One of the laziest phrases in research criticism is “this paper has issues.”

Sometimes that means the paper contains an obvious defect. Sometimes it means the paper omitted information needed for verification. Sometimes it means the critic is uneasy but refuses to state why.

Those are not the same thing.

If the scholarly record is going to become more reliable, it needs better labels and clearer commitments. Referee begins with a simpler distinction than ordinary academic conversation usually offers: defect or audit gap.

A defect is a detected weakness

A defect means the available evidence supports the presence of a specific research weakness.

The paper reports counts that do not reconcile with the stated sample size. The test set appears contaminated. The paper uses a statistical procedure that does not fit the structure of the data. The reported measurement is physiologically implausible. The paper’s stated evidence cannot support the claim it makes.

In those cases, the criticism is not merely that the paper is hard to evaluate. The criticism is that the paper appears to contain an actual reliability-relevant flaw.

That should be visible in the record.

Traditional peer review often fails at exactly this point. A reviewer notices a defect and mentions it in prose. Another reviewer misses it. The editor discounts it. The paper is published anyway. The published record then carries almost no lasting metadata about the weakness.

Referee is built around the opposite idea. If a defect is detected, it should become structured metadata attached to the paper.

An audit gap is also a weakness

An audit gap is different, but it also matters.

An audit gap means the paper does not provide enough information to verify a reliability-relevant claim. The exclusion criteria are not disclosed. The preprocessing steps are missing. The data split is described too vaguely to rule out leakage. The code is unavailable for a claim that depends on unreproduced computation. The paper withholds information required to understand what was done.

An audit gap does not prove the underlying work was wrong.

But it does prove that downstream users are being asked to trust what they cannot inspect.

That is not a minor reporting nuisance. In many settings it is a core reliability problem. A claim that cannot be audited should not receive the same default trust as a claim that can.

This is one reason Referee is useful outside academia. Investors, policymakers, journalists, regulators, and civic users often have to rely on papers without rerunning the work themselves. For them, unverifiability is not an abstract concern. It is risk.

Why Referee does not keep an “unresolved” scoring state

This is the main design choice.

Many evaluation systems want a third bucket called “unresolved.” It sounds nuanced. It sounds responsible. Often it is neither.

In practice, “unresolved” tends to do three things.

It hides disagreement that should be visible. It delays criticism that should be public. It invites deference to some imagined future expert who will supposedly settle the matter later.

That is exactly the habit Referee is trying to break.

Referee preserves ambiguity, but it does not preserve ambiguity by creating a third scoring state. The ambiguity belongs in the surrounding record: evidence excerpts, scope notes, vote splits, model disagreements, challenge status, and version history. The scoring layer still makes a call on the relevant CRWE item.

A public yes or no is harder to ignore and easier to challenge than a cloud of carefully worded hesitation.

Binary does not mean simplistic

Critics sometimes hear “binary” and imagine crude oversimplification.

But a binary call on a named weakness is often more informative than the soft language academics use to avoid commitment.

Consider two statements.

“The paper raises some concerns.”

“The paper triggers CRWE category X because the reported counts do not reconcile with the stated sample size.”

The second statement is narrower, more contestable, and easier to improve. Someone can inspect the evidence and say the count reconciliation is wrong, or that the category was misapplied, or that the issue has been corrected in a later version. The first statement mostly radiates mood.

Referee chooses binary scoring for the same reason good accounting systems require explicit entries. A structured public record needs claims that can be audited.

Reality is not binary. Accountability often is.

How ambiguity is preserved without becoming a third state

Removing “unresolved” from the scoring layer does not mean pretending every call is equally clean.

Some calls will rest on strong direct evidence. Some will rest on weaker text extraction. Some will involve model disagreement. Some will be made under narrow scope. Some will later be overturned.

That variation should be visible.

Referee can preserve the strength of evidence, the scope of the evaluation, the vote pattern across models, the date of the evaluation, the later challenge history, and the exact text or table used to support the call. It can show whether a weakness was triggered by a direct contradiction, a missing disclosure, a deterministic consistency failure, or a narrower interpretive judgment.

That is real nuance.

What it does not need is a third public state that lets the system avoid saying what it thinks.

The score should reflect defects and audit gaps directly

A paper’s score is derived from the binary CRWE vector and the weighting vector.

That means defects and audit gaps can both reduce the score, but they do so through explicit categories rather than through vague atmosphere.

This matters because the scholarly record needs decomposition. A paper with a score of 61 should not merely be “a 61.” It should be a paper that triggered certain defects, certain audit gaps, under a named taxonomy and a named weighting system.

Without that decomposition, the score would become another black box.

With it, users can see whether the paper looks weak because of direct methodological flaws, because it is difficult to audit, or because several smaller weaknesses accumulated.

Precision lowers the temperature

One reason academics fear public reliability scoring is that criticism can become reputational theater.

That fear is not imaginary.

But the cure is not vagueness. The cure is precision.

Saying “this paper is unreliable” without structure is a smear. Saying “this paper triggers a named weakness because the reported sample counts do not match the denominators in Table 2” is different. Saying “this paper triggers an audit-gap weakness because it does not disclose the exclusion rule needed to reconstruct the analysis” is different again.

Precision moves the argument away from status and toward evidence.

That is one of Referee’s core bets: public criticism becomes less personal, not more, when it is forced into explicit categories and tied to inspectable support.

Forced calls create a better correction loop

A system that never commits to a claim is difficult to improve.

Benchmarking becomes fuzzy. Appeals become fuzzy. Corrections become fuzzy. Everything turns into interpretation of interpretation.

Forced yes or no calls create a cleaner learning loop. The system can be wrong in specific ways. Those errors can be measured. Weighting can be adjusted. Definitions can be tightened. Prompts can be improved. Deterministic checks can be added. Authors can contest particular findings instead of objecting to a general cloud of suspicion.

That is one reason Referee is intentionally a public project. Noise is part of improvement. A criticism that can be named can be challenged. A challenge that is explicit can improve the system.

Better labels make a better record

The existing scholarly record has remarkably thin reliability labels.

Published. Corrected. Retracted. Cited. Peer reviewed.

That is not enough.

A published paper can contain defects. An unretracted paper can be unreliable. A highly cited paper can still rest on audit gaps. A paper can be methodologically careful in one respect and poor in another.

Referee is trying to add a missing layer of public metadata.

The first step is better naming. The second step is explicit scoring. The third step is an evidence trail that anyone can inspect.

Defect or audit gap is not a final ontology for all scholarship. But it is already better than “this paper has issues,” and it fits the architecture Referee is actually building.

That is why Referee forces a call anyway.

posts.title: From PDF to Provenance: How a Referee Score Gets Made
posts.excerpt: Referee does not upload a paper to an AI model and ask for a vague review. It turns the paper into a structured record, runs narrow checks tied to the CRWE, records evidence, makes explicit yes/no calls, and preserves the provenance behind the score.
posts.content:
The easiest way to misunderstand Referee is to imagine a system that uploads a PDF and asks an AI model, “Review this paper.”

That would be a bad design.

It would automate the worst part of peer review: broad judgment, shifting criteria, invisible reasoning, and dependence on whoever happened to be asked. The only difference would be that the vague reviewer was a machine.

Referee is not built that way.

It starts from three premises. First, most academics do not have the time, and often do not have the incentive, to perform line-by-line reliability review. Second, no peer reviewer is an expert in every way research can fail. Third, AI systems and non-academic contributors can make useful judgments when the task is narrow enough. Asking for a global verdict is too broad. Asking whether a paper discloses exclusion criteria, leaks the test set, or reports physiologically implausible values is a different kind of task.

That is the design logic behind Referee.

Step one: ingest the paper properly

Most papers still arrive as PDFs.

PDFs are fine for human reading and bad for structured evaluation. They flatten methods, tables, figures, metadata, and claims into a format that was never designed for machine-readable reliability analysis.

So the first task is ingestion.

Referee extracts text, metadata, identifiers, tables where possible, and document structure. It checks extraction quality because a reliability system that cannot reliably read the paper cannot reliably score the paper.

This step is not glamorous, but it is foundational. Garbage ingestion produces garbage criticism.

Step two: map the paper to explicit weakness families

The next step is not “decide what is wrong.”

The next step is “decide what kinds of weaknesses are relevant here.”

A randomized clinical study, a machine learning paper, a meta-analysis, and a qualitative field study do not expose the same failure modes. The CRWE exists precisely to stop evaluation from collapsing everything into a single complaint.

Referee uses the paper type, methods, claims, and extracted evidence to determine which weakness families are in scope. A missing train-test split is relevant to some papers and meaningless for others. A preregistration weakness may matter in one context and not in another. Applicability is part of the record.

This is where the project benefits from taxonomy. Once the weakness classes are named, evaluation can be organized.

Step three: extract evidence before interpreting it

Bad review systems jump straight to conclusions.

Referee should not.

For each relevant CRWE item, the system first tries to extract structured evidence. That evidence might be a sample size, a disclosed exclusion rule, a code availability statement, a train-validation-test description, a statistical procedure, a denominator, a range of reported values, or the absence of a required disclosure.

The order matters.

A reader should not be asked to trust a conclusion with no visible basis. The evidence should come first. The judgment should come second.

Traditional peer review often leaves only a sentence such as “the methods are insufficiently described.” That may be true, but it is not very reusable. Referee aims to preserve the more useful form of the claim: which CRWE category was implicated, what evidence was found, what was missing, and where the system looked.

Step four: add deterministic checks where possible

Not every evaluation step should rely on model interpretation.

Some things can be computed or checked more directly. Counts can sometimes be reconciled. Numerical claims can sometimes be checked for internal consistency. Reported measurements can sometimes be compared with known plausible ranges. Machine learning workflows can sometimes be checked for obvious leakage patterns or validation errors. Registry records and disclosures can sometimes be cross-checked.

These deterministic enrichments do not eliminate judgment, but they reduce dependence on model vibes.

That matters because models can overread ambiguous language or miss an obvious inconsistency. Computed context gives the later judgment phase firmer ground.

The right architecture is not “ask a chatbot.” It is structured extraction, deterministic enrichment where possible, narrow judgment where necessary, and provenance throughout.

Step five: ask narrow questions, not grand ones

This is the central move.

Referee does not ask whether the paper is good. It asks narrower questions tied to the CRWE.

Did the paper trigger the weakness “undisclosed exclusion criteria”? Did the paper trigger the weakness “code unavailable for a claim that depends on unreproduced computation”? Did the paper trigger the weakness “test-set contamination”? Did the paper trigger the weakness “implausible reported physiology”? Did the paper trigger the weakness “incompatible statistical test”?

These are not the same question. That is why they should not be merged into one open-ended review prompt.

Narrow questions make AI judgment more useful, and they create room for non-academic participation. Someone does not need to be the world authority on an entire field to evaluate a specific, well-defined weakness class.

Step six: make the call and preserve the disagreement

At some point the system has to decide.

For each CRWE item in scope, Referee records a yes or no. A yes means the weakness was triggered. A no means it was not triggered under the evidence and the rules used.

This is where many evaluation systems retreat into ambiguity. Referee should not.

Additional passes may be triggered when evidence is thin, the extraction is noisy, or models disagree. Multiple models may vote independently. Deterministic checks may add context. But the objective is still to produce an explicit call, not to hand difficult cases to some later tribunal of domain experts.

Disagreement is still important. It should be preserved. A unanimous call is different from a disputed one. A judgment based on clean evidence is different from one made under partial ambiguity. That information belongs in the provenance record.

What disagreement should not do is dissolve the scoring layer into “unresolved.”

Step seven: compute the score from the binary record

The score comes after the record.

Once the CRWE vector has been filled, Referee combines that binary vector with a versioned weighting vector. Higher weights mean more severe weaknesses. The result is a 0 to 100 reliability score derived from explicit findings rather than institutional prestige.

This order is critical.

The score is not a free-floating opinion. It is a summary of the named weaknesses that were or were not triggered. If the taxonomy changes, the score can change. If the evidence changes, the score can change. If a bad extraction produced a false positive and the record is corrected, the score can change.

But those changes should be traceable.

A score that moves without provenance is just another authority signal. A score that moves with provenance becomes part of a living scholarly record.

Step eight: preserve the audit trail

The most important output may not be the number.

It may be the trail that explains how the number was produced.

For Referee, that means preserving extraction outputs, evidence snippets, deterministic checks, model calls, disagreement patterns, timestamps, taxonomy version, scoring version, and the history of later challenges or corrections.

That is not only good engineering. It is part of the project’s logic.

Referee is not trying to replace one black box with another. It is trying to make criticism inspectable. If the evaluation cannot itself be evaluated, it has failed.

The point is not expert triage

Many systems are built to identify “interesting” cases and then pass them to experts.

That is a reasonable design for some workflows. It is not the central point of Referee.

Referee exists to produce structured reliability metadata directly. The project is not merely a queue for later judgment. It is a public record of explicit weakness calls tied to evidence and versioned scoring.

People can dispute those calls. Authors can contest them. New evidence can change them. But the system should still say something now.

A reliability layer that only points elsewhere has not yet become infrastructure.

That is the path from PDF to provenance.

posts.title: What a Referee Score Actually Measures
posts.excerpt: The scholarly record already scores papers through prestige, reputation, and citation habits. Referee replaces those hidden scores with an explicit, versioned measure based on named research weaknesses, public evidence, and decomposable scoring.
posts.content:
People object to paper scoring as if numbers are the thing making scholarship reductive.

That is backwards.

The scholarly record already scores papers. It just does so through signals it refuses to name. Journal brand functions as a score. Author prestige functions as a score. Institutional status functions as a score. Citation counts function as a score. Famous labs receive one level of default trust and unknown authors receive another.

Those signals shape what gets read, funded, cited, defended, and translated into policy.

The problem is not that scholarship has scores. The problem is that its scores are mostly unofficial, opaque, and tied to status rather than inspectable evidence.

Referee makes the scoring layer explicit.

Not truth, not novelty, not prestige

A Referee score is not a truth score.

It does not tell you that a paper is correct. It does not tell you that a result will replicate everywhere. It does not tell you that a theory is important, interesting, elegant, or career-making. It does not tell you that the paper deserves a prize, a grant, or a place in the canon.

It is also not a novelty score. The project is not trying to reward surprise. A paper can be novel and unreliable. It can also be dull and solid.

It is not a citation score. Citation counts capture visibility, fashion, controversy, and social copying at least as much as quality.

It is not a moral score. A low score does not mean the authors are bad actors. Most weak papers are not fraudulent. They are underpowered, under-described, over-claimed, over-fit, confounded, or produced inside institutions that reward publishing more than verification.

A Referee score measures something narrower and more useful: the structural reliability of the paper under a named set of checks.

The score is built from named weaknesses

Referee starts from a simple claim: many research failures recur. They are not infinite. They are not mysterious. They can be named, defined, checked, and recorded.

That is the purpose of the Common Research Weakness Enumeration, or CRWE.

The CRWE is an enumeration of the ways research can be unreliable. Some weaknesses concern design. Some concern missing disclosures. Some concern statistical reasoning. Some concern reproducibility. Some concern machine learning practice, laboratory procedure, causal inference, measurement, qualitative methods, or formal argument.

The point is not to pretend that all papers are the same. The point is to stop treating reliability concerns as vague vibes.

A Referee score is computed from a binary weakness vector and a versioned weighting vector. For each CRWE item that is in scope, the evaluation records a yes or a no. A yes means the weakness was triggered. A no means it was not triggered under the available evidence and the rules applied. In simplified form, the reliability score is one minus the sum product of that binary CRWE vector and the weighting vector, expressed on a 0 to 100 scale. Larger weights represent more severe weaknesses. All weights are positive. If a category matters so little that its weight should be zero, it should not be in the enumeration.

The number is therefore not a mood. It is a summary of explicit claims.

Why Referee forces a call

This is an intentional design choice.

Referee does not use “unresolved” as a third scoring state. Ambiguity is real, but ambiguity belongs in the evidence trail, scope statement, vote splits, challenge history, and version record. The public scoring layer still makes a yes or no call on named weaknesses.

Why force the call?

Because a system that never commits to a claim is hard to correct. It can only gesture. It cannot be cleanly challenged. It cannot be benchmarked. It cannot create the public friction that improvement requires.

The scholarly record already has too much ambiguity hiding behind prestige. Referee is trying to create less.

A binary call is not a claim that reality is simple. It is a claim that public accountability requires explicit judgments.

The number is the summary, not the product

The score matters because people need compression.

No policymaker, investor, editor, journalist, researcher, or citizen can inspect every methodological detail of every paper they encounter. A summary layer is unavoidable. The real question is whether the summary is tied to evidence or to status.

Referee’s answer is that the number should be the entry point, not the whole product.

The actual product is the record behind the number: which CRWE items were in scope, which ones were triggered, what evidence was extracted, which deterministic checks ran, which models voted, how the weights were applied, and which taxonomy version produced the result.

A score without provenance is just another prestige proxy.

A Referee score should always be unpackable. The reader should be able to move from the number to the weakness categories, from the categories to the specific finding, from the finding to the evidence, and from the evidence back to the paper.

What a high score means

A high score means the paper triggered relatively few or relatively low-severity weaknesses among the checks that were applied.

That is a useful signal.

It does not mean the paper is correct. It does not mean every weakness category on earth was evaluated. It does not mean the theory matters. It does not mean the result will generalize. It does not mean future evidence will never change the record.

It means something narrower: under the versioned CRWE, the weighting vector, and the evidence scope used for this evaluation, the paper did not trigger many serious weaknesses.

That is already more informative than journal prestige.

What a low score means

A low score means the paper triggered enough weaknesses, or enough severe weaknesses, to materially reduce confidence in its structural reliability.

Those weaknesses can arise in more than one way.

Some are defects. The paper appears to contain an actual reliability-relevant flaw. Some are audit gaps. The paper withholds or omits information needed to verify a reliability-relevant claim. Both matter. A paper can be unreliable because the work is weak. It can also be unreliable because the work cannot be audited from what is shown.

Referee does not collapse those cases into one vague complaint. It names them.

That matters because downstream users need to know not only that confidence should fall, but why.

This is for society, not just academia

Referee is not being built only for journal editors or tenure committees.

The broader audience is everyone forced to rely on research without having the time, incentives, or specialized training to re-review it from scratch: policymakers, diligence teams, investors, journalists, practitioners, nonprofits, civic groups, and ordinary readers.

Academia can participate, and some academics will be excellent contributors. But the project does not assume that academia alone will solve the reliability problem. In many cases it plainly has not.

Referee exists because the scholarly record needs a reliability layer that is more public, more explicit, and less deferential.

The status quo is more reductive than the score

The usual criticism is that a single number is too simple.

Sometimes that criticism is right. A bad score can mislead.

But the alternative is not rich nuance delivered by impartial experts. The actual alternative is a mix of journal brand, reviewer lottery, institutional prestige, intellectual fashion, and the social habit of trusting whatever everyone else already trusts.

Compared with that system, a transparent score tied to named weaknesses and inspectable evidence is not less nuanced. It is more nuanced.

The score is the front door. The evidence record is the building.

That is what a Referee score actually measures.

posts.title: Academic collegiality is a bug, not a feature (and it's largely not true)
posts.excerpt: Academia claims to value collegiality, yet the peer review system reveals a cutthroat reality marked by retaliation fears, competitive scooping, and reluctance to provide thorough reviews. Referee proposes removing the 'peer' from peer review, democratizing the process beyond credentialed gatekeepers to enable direct, unfiltered criticism that strengthens research evaluation.
posts.content:
When I listen to people talk about improving peer review, I often detect a paradox: the importance of embracing academic collegiality while acknowledging the cutthroat nature of academia. They highlight concerns about potential retaliation if peer reviewer anonymity were compromised and relate researcher worries about competitors scooping their preprints. In addition, all the evidence suggests that most academics are reluctant to help improve their fellow colleagues's work by conducting peer reviews, often declining opportunities, procrastinating on them, or increasingly relying on generative AI to hasten their reviews. And let's not ignore the use of paper mills and brokers that poison the research record. It's evident that while academic collegiality is an admirable goal, it often conflicts with the real-world experiences of academics.

This dichotomy not only undercuts the idealistic image of a collegial academic environment but also signals a critical flaw in the peer review structure. As Simine Vazire, professor of psychology at the University of Melbourne and editor-in-chief of Psychological Science, conceded on a Freaknomics podcast, "Our field doesn’t have a culture of open criticism. It’s not considered okay." If open criticism is not a foundational element of an evaluation system, then how robust is the system in the first place? If open criticism—a cornerstone of scientific inquiry—is self-suppressed, can we truly trust the robustness of the academic peer review system?

At Referee, we recognize these issues and propose a necessary shift. Traditional peer review, relying on the voluntary contributions of overburdened academics, fails to reach its potential. Our approach takes the peer out of peer review. This is not just some provocative slogan but a necessary condition to improve integrity of research evaluation. By democratizing peer review beyond the ivory tower, we remove significant barriers that hinder the advancement of human knowledge. We integrate structured, anonymous, and professionalized review processes, protecting reviewers from the pressures of academic rivalry and personal bias, ensuring that the reliability of papers are evaluated purely on their merits.

There is a vital space in the knowledge curation landscape for direct, unfiltered criticism, and that is where Referee steps in. We welcome the expertise of academics but believe that democratizing the review process is essential for a truly robust and fair academic discourse. Of course, we encourage academics to participate, but we reject the need for a gated community of credentialed peers.

Come join us. Let’s not merely patch a failing system—let’s rebuild it for a new era of transparency and fairness in academic rigor.

posts.title: Gaming Referee
posts.content:
At Referee, we not only expect the system to be gamed, we encourage it! Why? It stems from our belief that adversarial processes make the strongest systems. By encouraging and anticipating how the system can be gamed early, we hope to build a robust system that engenders trust across all stakeholders. After all, we take a hacker’s mindset to the problem of research evaluation, so why not embrace the approach to our own system?

This is a living document that will continue to add possible ways the system can be gamed and how we would approach them.

 

Collusive Reviewing

  • Technique: A group of validators might collude to consistently approve each other's bounty claims, regardless of their validity.
  • Solution: Implement a random assignment of validators to claims and introduce periodic audits of validator activities by independent auditors.

 

Sybil Attacks

  • Technique: An individual could create multiple accounts to influence the system, either to push through fraudulent claims or to manipulate reputation scores.
  • Solution: Require robust identity verification processes for each account to ensure one person cannot control multiple accounts.

 

Pseudo-Flaw Flooding

  • Technique: Flooding the system with trivial or borderline irrelevant flaws to claim bounties, thus diluting the focus on more significant issues.
  • Solution: Develop a tiered system for flaws based on their impact on the paper's integrity, adjusting bounty rewards accordingly.

 

Reputation Laundering

  • Technique: Validators or bounty hunters artificially inflate their reputation through minor but numerous successful bounty claims.
  • Solution: Introduce weighting mechanisms that give more importance to the impact of each claim on scientific discourse rather than just the number of successful claims.

 

Echo Chamber Creation

  • Technique: A partisan group could dominate certain topics or fields, pushing their biased views by selectively approving bounties that align with their stance.
  • Solution: Ensure diversity in the validator pool and set up mechanisms that require consensus among validators from different backgrounds.

 

Bounty Claim Hoarding

  • Technique: Bounty hunters could hoard information about significant flaws to claim multiple bounties before others notice.
  • Solution: Set a time limit for holding off on public disclosure after the initial discovery of a flaw, encouraging timely reporting.

 

Bribery and Corruption

  • Technique: Bounty providers could bribe validators to approve claims on their papers, or validators could solicit bribes in exchange for claim approval.
  • Solution: Implement strict anti-corruption policies and penalties, including permanent bans for bribery.

 

Data Poisoning

  • Technique: Introducing subtly incorrect data or references in papers that can mislead automated scanners and validators.
  • Solution: Combine automated tools with expert human review to catch nuanced manipulations not detected by AI.

 

Targeted Smear Campaigns

  • Technique: Using the platform to systematically lower the reliability scores of researchers, institutions or ideologies through coordinated attacks.
  • Solution: Monitor for abnormal patterns in bounty submissions and validations, possibly integrating a peer-review model among validators to check each other’s work.

 

Exploitation of Model Weaknesses

  • Technique: Identifying and exploiting specific weaknesses in the AI models used for initial paper scans to manipulate review outcomes.
  • Solution: Continuously update and test AI models against known exploitation techniques and encourage the community to report new vulnerabilities.
posts.excerpt: At Referee, we embrace the hacker's mindset: anticipating and encouraging attempts to game the system makes it stronger. This living document explores potential attack vectors—from collusive reviewing to reputation laundering—and outlines robust countermeasures that build trust through adversarial resilience.

posts.title: No AI Agents, No Scalability in Research Validation
posts.excerpt: With academics spending 100 million hours annually on peer review yet still unable to keep pace with millions of new papers, AI agents offer the only scalable solution. By focusing AI on granular, specific research weaknesses rather than holistic reviews, Referee enables specialized detection that overcomes concerns about context, hallucinations, and subjective judgments.
posts.content:
Researchers have estimated that academics collectively spent over 100 million hours (~15,000 people years) reviewing ~4.7 million manuscripts and pre-prints every year for possible publishing in 30,000 scientific journals. That research only considered papers submitted to journals and did not consider the millions of papers that are posted to various preprint repositories. When those are considered, it becomes clear that many more reviewers are needed to ensure the scholarly record has been properly vetted.

The Challenge of Finding Qualified Reviewers

Will academia find enough qualified researchers to fill the gap? If history is any guide, then the answer is no. Decades of evidence suggests that many academics are not motivated to conduct thorough peer reviews, and it’s easy to understand why. There are no career incentives that reward such activity and there may be retributions for particularly critical reviews. This is one reason why many reviewers are anonymous. Furthermore, the PhD and tenure processes screen for people more interested in the hard work of conducting and publishing papers based on new research than in reviewing the work of others. With little chance of these processes changing, the gap remains.

Introducing AI into the Peer Review Process

Implementing AI agents to review pre-print papers can address this gap, but such adoption faces resistance. The main objections include the ability of agents to truly understand the wider context that the paper exists within, their known tendency to hallucinate, and the problem of weaknesses that don’t lend themselves to binary true/false answers. Those are valid complaints, but only as applied to the traditional holistic reviews. Referee’s approach of decomposing research weaknesses to a granular level enables specialized generative AI agents to both identify paper weaknesses and to evaluate submitted bounty claims of weaknesses. This is the needed breakthrough that allows a more complete assessment of the scholarly record. Let’s address each of these complaints in detail.

Understanding Research Context

Publishing a paper is a contribution to a running conversation held within that specific domain. It considers the prior arguments that have been presented (often references in the background and literature review section) and builds on or challenges specific conclusions made. The strength and importance of the paper’s conclusions are often evaluated as part of the traditional peer review process. This is a valuable assessment that requires years of experience and familiarity with the subject matter. However, this is not the focus of Referee reviews. Referee is only concerned with the reliability of the research presented and does not consider paper importance nor ethical considerations. We see traditional journals keeping their roles in evaluating these dimensions of research. When the focus is only on reliability, great progress can be made.

Referee’s common research weakness enumeration (CRWE) is a granular taxonomy of paper flaws that allows humans are AI agents to develop specialized capabilities in detecting specific weaknesses. An analogy can be made to economies. At one time, individuals and extended family units had to provide for all their needs, from food and shelter to clothing and medical aid. Once specialization was introduced, however, economies began to grow expansively. The same expansion can occur in research evaluation. Not every academic is an expert on the latest statistical techniques or methodological approaches, yet they are asked to become such experts when requested to review papers. This introduces numerous challenges, challenges that muddy the quality of the scholarly record.

Generative AI has proven its capability to quickly analyze large volumes of data and identify specific inconsistencies or errors. Can they be trusted to produce results with the needed accuracy? With the right instructions, yes. The key is focusing them on a specific topic. In the early stages, AI agents will have to be carefully monitored to ensure consistency and correctness. Over time, the best AI agents can be whitelisted to reduce monitoring burden.

Understanding Research Context

What about hallucinations? Like humans, any claim submitted by AI agents must be approved by validators. Requiring a small fee for submissions makes it (very) costly for developers to have their agents submit poor and inaccurate claims. AI agents are also a necessity on the bounty validation side as well, a task they are well-suited for. Generative AI are consensus machines and are good at identifying Schelling Points (i.e. the most likely answer to a question when people cannot communicate). Stated simply, they can reliably state whether the provided evidence meets the criteria for a specific research weakness. Hallucinations can be overcome by adding multiple bots to a jury pool as while they hallucinate, they are highly unlikely to do it at the same time. Agents from different models can further reduce the risk of training set biases.

Discretionary Judgements in Peer Review

The final objection to the use of AI agents is that many questions of research weaknesses don’t lend themselves to binary conclusions like vulnerabilities do in cybersecurity. But how are these tricky questions handled today? Reviewers applying variable personal thresholds that are unobservable does not promote transparency or consistency. And while AI agents can also be opaque in their decisions, their temperature can be adjusted and the use of multi-agent juries would improve the robustness of decisions. As the CRWE improves in its granularity and criteria, so will the consistency of AI agent improve. Reasonable people can debate the criteria but such debates are exactly the type of meta-discussions that academia should be having regarding research reliability. A discussion is best left to human experts.

Conclusion: Embracing AI in Academic Reviewing

The integration of AI agents into the Referee ecosystem represents a lengthy yet transformative process that holds great promise for enhancing the quality and transparency of the scholarly record. By leveraging the precision and analytical capabilities of AI, academia can address the persistent challenges of peer review efficiency and bias. This technological shift not only aims to reduce the heavy workload on academics but also fosters a more dynamic and robust scholarly environment. As AI technologies improve and adapt, they could also facilitate a more inclusive and diverse academic dialogue by removing geographical and institutional barriers, ultimately enriching the global exchange of knowledge. Society should not only welcome this development but actively support and participate in its refinement and implementation.
